html:
II

While Theodore Savage paid his court to Phillida Rathbone, the Karthanian decision was the subject of more than conversation; diplomatists and statesmen were busy while he drifted into love and dreamed through the sudden rumours that excited his fellows at the office. In London, for the most part, journalism was guarded and reticent, the threat of secession at first hardly mentioned; but in nations and languages that favoured secession the press was voicing the popular cry with enthusiasm that grew daily more heated. Through conflicting rumour this at least was clear: at the next meeting of the Council of the League its authority would be tested to the uttermost, since the measure of independent action demanded by the malcontent members would amount to a denial of the federal principle, to secession in fact if not in name.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Reaction against central and unified authority was not a phenomenon of yesterday; it had been gathering its strength through years of racial friction, finding an adherent in every community that considered itself aggrieved by a decision of the Council or award of the Court of Arbitration, and for years it had taxed the ingenuity of the majority of the Council to avoid open breach and defiance.

Before open breach and its consequences, both sides had so far manoeuvred, hesitated, compromised; it had been left to a minor, a very minor, state, to rush in where others feared to tread. The flat refusal of a heady, half-civilized little democracy to accept the unfavourable verdict of the Court of Arbitration was the spark that might fire a powder-barrel; its frothy demonstrations, ridiculous in themselves, appealed to the combative instinct in others, to race-hatreds, old herding feuds and jealousies. These found vent in answering demonstrations, outbursts of popular sympathy in states not immediately affected; the noisy rebel was hailed as a martyr and pioneer of freedom, and became the pretext for resistance to the CouncilвАЩs oppression. There was no doubt of the extent of the regrouping movement of the nations, of the stirrings of a widespread combativeness which denounced Federation as a system whereby dominant interests and races exploited their weaker rivals. With the meeting of the Council would come the inevitable clash of interests; the summons to the offending member of the League to retreat from its impossible position, andвБ†вАФin case of continued defianceвБ†вАФthe proposal to take punitive action. That proposal, to all seeming, must bring about a crisis; those members of the League who had encouraged the rebel in defiance would hardly consent to cooperate in punitive measures; and refusalвБ†вАФwithdrawal of their military contingentsвБ†вАФwould mean virtual secession and denial of majority rule. If collective excitement and anger ran high, it might mean even more than secession; there were possibilitiesвБ†вАФfirst hinted at, later discussed without subterfugeвБ†вАФof actual and armed opposition should the Council attempt to enforce its decree and authority.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Humanity, once more, was gathering into herds and growing sharply conscious alike of division and comradeship.

It was some time before Theodore was even touched by the herding instinct and spirit; apart, in a delicate world of his own, he concerned himself even less than usual with the wider interests of politics. By his fellows in the Distribution Office he was known as an incurable optimist; even when the cloud had spread rapidly and darkened he saw вАЬstrained relationsвАЭ through the eyes of a lover, and his mind, busied elsewhere, refused to dwell anxiously on вАЬincidentsвАЭ and вАЬdisquieting possibilities.вАЭ They intruded clumsily on his delicate world and, so soon as might be, he thrust them behind him and slipped back to the seclusion that belonged to himself and a woman. All his life, thought and impulse, for the time being, was a negation, a refusal of the idea of strife and destruction; in his happy egoism he planned to make and buildвБ†вАФa home and a lifetime of content.

Now and again, and in spite of his reluctance, his veil of happy egoism was brushed asideвБ†вАФsome chance word or incident forcing him to look upon the menace. There was the evening in VallanceвАЩs rooms, for instanceвБ†вАФwhere the talk settled down to the political crisis, and Holt, the long journalist, turned sharply on Vallance, who supposed we were drifting into war.

вАЬThatвАЩs nonsense, Vallance! Nonsense! ItвАЩs impossibleвБ†вАФunthinkable!вАЭ

вАЬUnpleasant, if you like,вАЭ said Vallance; вАЬbut not impossible. At leastвБ†вАФit never has been.вАЭ

вАЬThatвАЩs no reason,вАЭ Holt retorted; вАЬweвАЩre not living yesterday. ThereвАЩll be no war, and IвАЩll tell you why: because the men who will have to start itвБ†вАФdarenвАЩt!вАЭ He had a penetrating voice which he raised when excited, so that other talk died down and the room was filled with his argument. Politicians, he insisted, might bluff and use threatsвБ†вАФmenace with a bogey, shake a weapon they dared not useвБ†вАФbut they would stop short at threats, manoeuvre for position and retreat. Let loose modern science, mechanics and chemistry, they could notвБ†вАФthere was a limit to human insanity, if only because there was a limit to the endurance of the soldier. Unless you supposed that all politicians were congenital idiots or criminal lunatics out to make holocausts. What was happening at present was manoeuvring pure and simple; neither side caring to prejudice its case by open admission that appeal to force was unthinkable, each side hoping that the other would be the first to make the admission, each side trotting out the dummy soldiers that were only for show, and would soon be put back in their boxes.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ War, he repeated, was unthinkable.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

вАЬMan,вАЭ said a voice behind Theodore, вАЬdoes much that is unthinkable!вАЭ

Theodore turned that he might look at the speakerвБ†вАФMarkham, something in the scientific line, who had sat in silence, with a pipe between his lips, till he dropped out his slow remark.

вАЬYour mistake,вАЭ he went on, вАЬlies in taking these peopleвБ†вАФstatesmen, politiciansвБ†вАФfor free agents, and in thinking they have only one fear. Look at MeyerвАЩs speech this morningвБ†вАФthatвАЩs significant. He has been moderate so far, a restraining influence; now he breathes fire and throws in his lot with the extremists. What do you make of that?вАЭ

вАЬMerely,вАЭ said Holt, вАЬthat Meyer has lost his head.вАЭ

вАЬIn which happy state,вАЭ suggested Vallance, вАЬthe impossible and unthinkable maynвАЩt frighten him.вАЭ

вАЬThatвАЩs one explanation,вАЭ said Markham. вАЬThe other is that he is divided between his two fearsвБ†вАФthe fear of war and the fear of his democracy, which, being in a quarrelsome and restless mood, would break him if he flinched and applauds him to the echo when he blusters. And, maybe, at the moment, his fear of being broken is greater than his fear of the impossibleвБ†вАФat any rate the threat is closer.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ The man himself may be reasonableвБ†вАФeven nowвБ†вАФbut he is the instrument of instinctive emotion. Almost any man, taken by himself, is reasonableвБ†вАФand, being reasonable, cautious. Meyer can think, just as well as you and I, so long as he stands outside a crowd; but neither you nor I, nor Meyer, can think when we are one with thousands and our minds are absorbed into a jelly of impulse and emotion.вАЭ

вАЬI like your phrase about jelly,вАЭ said Vallance. вАЬIt has an odd picturesqueness. Your argument itselfвБ†вАФor, rather, your assertionвБ†вАФstrikes me as a bit sweeping.вАЭ

вАЬAll the same,вАЭ Markham nodded, вАЬitвАЩs worth thinking over.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Man in the mass, as a crowd, can only feel; there is no such thing as a mass-mind or intellectвБ†вАФonly mass desires and emotions. That is what I mean by saying that MeyerвБ†вАФwhatever his intelligence or sanityвБ†вАФis the instrument of instinctive emotion.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ And instinctive emotion, HoltвБ†вАФuntil it has been hurtвБ†вАФis damnably and owlishly courageous. It isnвАЩt clever enough to be afraid; not even of red murderвБ†вАФor starvation by the millionвБ†вАФor the latest thing in gas or high explosive. Stir it up enough and itвАЩll run on вАЩemвБ†вАФas the lemmings run to the sea.вАЭ

Holt snorted something that sounded like вАЬRot!вАЭ and Vallance, sprawling an arm along the mantelpiece, asked, вАЬAnother of your numerous theories?вАЭ

вАЬIf you like,вАЭ Markham assented, вАЬbut itвАЩs a theory deduced from hard facts.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ ItвАЩs a fact, isnвАЩt it, that no politician takes a crowd into his confidence until he wants to make a fight of it? ItвАЩs a fact, isnвАЩt it, that no movements in mass are creative or constructiveвБ†вАФthat simultaneous action, simultaneous thought, always is and must be destructive? Set what we call the People in motion and something has got to be broken. The crowd-life is still at the elementary, the animal stage; it has not yet acquired the human power of constructionвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ and the crowd, the people, democracyвБ†вАФwhatever you like to call itвБ†вАФhas been stirring in the last few years; getting conscious again, getting active, looking round for something to breakвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ which means that the politician is faced once more with the necessity of giving it something to break. Naturally he prefers that the breakage should take place in the distanceвБ†вАФand, League or no League, the eternal and obvious resource is WarвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ which was not too risky when fought with swords and muskets, but nowвБ†вАФas Holt saysвБ†вАФis impossible. Being a bit of a chemist, IвАЩm sure Holt is right; but IвАЩm also sure that man, as a herd, does not think. Further, I am doubtful if man, as a herd, ever finds out what is impossible except through the painful process of breaking his head against it.вАЭ

вАЬIвАЩm a child in politics,вАЭ said Vallance, вАЬand I may be denseвБ†вАФbut IвАЩm afraid it isnвАЩt entirely clear to me whether your views are advanced or grossly and shamelessly reactionary?вАЭ

вАЬNeither,вАЭ said Markham, вАЬor bothвБ†вАФyou can take your choice. I have every sympathy with the people, the multitude; itвАЩs hard lines that it can only achieve destructionвБ†вАФjust because there is so much of it, because it isnвАЩt smaller. But I also sympathize with the politician in his efforts to control the destructive impulse of the multitude. And, finallyвБ†вАФin view of that progress of science of which Holt has reminded us, and of which I know a little myselfвБ†вАФIвАЩm exceedingly sorry for us all.вАЭ

Someone from across the room asked: вАЬYou make it war, then?вАЭ

вАЬI make it war. We have had peace for more than a generation, so our periodic bloodletting is already a long time overdue. The League has staved it off for a bit, but it hasnвАЩt changed the human constitution; and the real factor in the Karthanian quarrelвБ†вАФor any otherвБ†вАФis the periodic need of the human herd for something to break and for something to break itself against.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Resistance and self-sacrificeвБ†вАФthe need of themвБ†вАФthe call of the lemming to the sea.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ And, perhaps, itвАЩs all the stronger in this generation because this generation has never known war, and does not fear it.вАЭ

вАЬEducation,вАЭ said Holt, addressing the air, вАЬis general and compulsoryвБ†вАФhas been so for a good many years. The inference being that the records of previous warsвБ†вАФand incidentally of the devastation involvedвБ†вАФare not inaccessible to that large proportion of our population which is known as the average man.вАЭ

вАЬAs printed pages, yes,вАЭ Markham agreed. вАЬBut what proportion even of a literate population is able to accept the statement of a printed page as if it were a personal experience?вАЭ

вАЬAs weвАЩre not all fools,вАЭ Holt retorted, вАЬI donвАЩt make it war.вАЭ

вАЬI hope youвАЩre right, for my own sake,вАЭ said Markham good-temperedly. He knocked out his pipe as he spoke and made ready to goвБ†вАФwhile Theodore looked after him, interested, for the moment, disturbingly.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ MarkhamвАЩs unemotional and matter-of-fact acceptance of вАЬperiodic bloodlettingвАЭ made rumour suddenly real, and for the first time Theodore saw the Karthanian imbroglio as more than the substance of telegrams and articles, something human, actual, and alive.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Saw himself, even Phillida, concerned in itвБ†вАФthrough a medley of confused and threatening shadows.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ For the moment he was roused from his self-absorption and thrust into the world that he shared with the common herd of men. He and Phillida were no longer as the gods apart, with their lives to make in Eden; they were little human beings, the sport of a common human destiny.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ He remembered how eagerly he caught at HoltвАЩs condemnation of Markham as a crank and VallanceвАЩs next comment on the crisis.

вАЬWe had exactly the same scare threeвБ†вАФor was it four?вБ†вАФyears ago. This is the trouble about Transylvania all over againвБ†вАФjust the same alarums and excursions. That fizzled out quietly in a month or six weeks and the chances are that Karthania will fizzle out, too.вАЭ

вАЬOf course it will,вАЭ Holt declared with emphasisвБ†вАФand proceeded to demolish MarkhamвАЩs theories. Theodore left before he had finished his argument; as explained dogmatically in HoltвАЩs penetrating voice, the intrigues and dissensions of the Federal Council were once more unreal and frankly boring. The argument satisfied, but no longer interestedвБ†вАФand ten minutes after MarkhamвАЩs departure his thoughts had drifted away from politics to the private world he shared with Phillida Rathbone.

For very delight of it he lingered over his courtship, finding charm in the pretence of uncertainty long after it had ceased to exist. To Phillida also there was pleasure not only in the winning, but in the exquisite game itself; once or twice when Theodore was hovering near avowal, she deferred the inevitable, eluded him with laughter, asked tacitly to play a little longer.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ In the end the avowal came suddenly, on the flash and impulse of a momentвБ†вАФwhen Phillida hesitated over one of his gifts, a print she had admired on the wall of his sitting-room, duly brought the next day for her acceptance.

вАЬNo, I oughtnвАЩt to take itвБ†вАФitвАЩs one of your treasures,вАЭ she remonstrated.

вАЬIf youвАЩd take all I haveвБ†вАФand me with it,вАЭ he stammered.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ That was the crisis of the exquisite gameвБ†вАФand pretence of uncertainty was over.
